target:
SEC-6.14 (Part 6.14 κ Coupling Cluster) — insert a new dedicated

subsection "Why the Chebychev low-pass rescue fails" between the existing

What past SG data actually shows

subsection and

The vise

subsection. Engages the dome's strongest published-or-anticipated rebuttal — that standard SG Level-3 reduction pipelines digitally filter contact-phase transients out before publication — and shows why the rescue fails on four independent grounds. Closes ISS-1965 (carry-forward through cycles c1/c2/c3 of the SEC-6.14 review).
review_class_override_note: Tracker classified as deep-attack (baby-orphan-pickup default, ISS class_hint=deep-attack from PROP-027 routing at decider-2026-05-11T13-20Z). Authored EXP declares deep-attack: introduces a new <h3> subsection with four new substantive arguments — pipeline-band-corner analysis, raw-record archival counter, INTERMAGNET-side-symmetric reductio, and unfalsifiability/transfer-function critique — plus new citations (Boy & Hinderer 2006; IGETS/GGP raw archive; INTERMAGNET 1-minute definitive-data spec). Singleton curmudgeon review warranted.
scope_summary: ISS-1965 (severity=moderate, class_hint=deep-attack, routed 2026-05-11T13:22Z) tracks SEC-6.14.c1 H2: "INCOMPLETE ENGAGEMENT WITH STRONGEST DOME REBUTTAL — the 'What the dome could do' subsection lumps the Chebychev low-pass argument into a parenthetical of four post-hoc rescues and dismisses all four collectively." Curmudgeon cycles c1 (2026-05-11T05:18Z), c2 (2026-05-11T~10:20Z), and c3 (2026-05-11T13:22Z) all carry the hole forward unfixed. c3 recommendation: "Add one dedicated paragraph distinguishing Chebychev (a real methodological feature of Level-3 reduction) from the bedrock/noise/parameter-shift carve-outs (which lack independent grounding). Counter-engagement: (i) cite actual filter cutoff in Van Camp 2001 or Crossley intercomparison; (ii) note raw pre-filter residuals are publicly archived at GGP/IGETS; (iii) if no pulse appears even in raw data, the Chebychev counter fails on its own grounds. Engage on empirical merits rather than rhetorical dismissal." This EXP authors a dedicated <h3> subsection placed between "What past SG data actually shows" and "The vise" — explicit position so the reader sees the strongest-rebuttal engagement immediately after the null-data subsection and before the forced-choice argument. Four grounds of failure: (1) pipeline filter corners (microseism-suppression seconds, drift-removal >24h) do not touch the 1-min-to-3-hour band where κ predicts the pulse — and a filter that did would obliterate the K1/M2/free-oscillation signals the SG is designed to measure; (2) raw 1–2 Hz records are IGETS-archived for both Membach 1999 and the Chinese SG sites 2009, and the Mansinha 2001 noise floor on raw records is 30–900× below the predicted pulse; (3) symmetric reductio — the dome accepts 1-minute INTERMAGNET BOU products as κ's numerator, products which are themselves Gaussian-filtered, so the dome cannot consistently reject 1-minute SG products on filter grounds; (4) the rescue is unfalsifiable as published — the dome publishes no transfer function, no corner frequency, no site/time application list, no forward attenuation calculation. Engagement is honest about the kernel of truth (Level-3 SG pipelines do apply digital filters; this is real, not a strawman) before showing why the rescue still fails.
kernel_of_truth: Standard SG data-reduction pipelines genuinely DO apply digital filters before publishing Level-3 residuals. Boy & Hinderer 2006 documents the canonical SG tidal-residual processing chain; Crossley/Hinderer/Riccardi 2013 (Rep. Prog. Phys. 76, 046101) reviews the full pipeline; the IGETS processing manual (successor to the GGP standards) specifies Earth-tide-model subtraction, atmospheric-pressure-admittance correction at ~0.3 µGal/mbar, instrumental-drift removal at periods >24h, and microseism-suppression low-passes with corners at periods of a few seconds. A defender pointing out that Level-3 publications show post-pipeline residuals (not raw 1-2 Hz records) is methodologically correct. Lumping this with the bedrock-conductivity and instrumental-noise carve-outs in a parenthetical dismissal was rhetorical rather than empirical. The Chebychev rescue deserves separate engagement because it is the dome's strongest card on this exchange — and even the strongest card loses, because the pipeline's actual filter corners do not touch the band κ predicts, the raw records are archived independently and show no pulse, and the rescue is symmetric against the dome's own magnetometer-side numerator.

patches.anchor_uniqueness_note:
String "

The vise

" appears exactly once in part6.html (verified 2026-05-16T this run by counting occurrences). The preceding paragraph ends with "...the SGs that were sampling those eclipses would have recorded it. They did not.

" followed by a blank line and then the anchor. Insert the new

subsection AFTER that blank line and BEFORE "

The vise

".
patches.insert_html:
Why the Chebychev low-pass rescue fails

Of the four post-hoc rescues catalogued in What the dome could do below, the Chebychev low-pass argument deserves separate engagement because it has independent methodological grounding the others lack. Standard SG data-reduction pipelines genuinely do apply digital filters before publishing Level-3 residuals (see Boy & Hinderer 2006 on SG processing chains; Crossley, Hinderer & Riccardi 2013, Rep. Prog. Phys. 76, 046101 for the canonical pipeline description). A defender can correctly point out that Level-3 publications do not show the raw 1–2 Hz record; they show residuals after Earth-tide-model subtraction, atmospheric-pressure-admittance correction (~0.3 µGal/mbar), instrumental-drift removal at periods longer than ~24 h, and in some chains a digital low-pass with corners in the seconds-to-minute range to suppress microseismic and electronic noise. So far the rescue stands on solid methodological ground.

It fails on four grounds. First, the published filter corners do not touch the contact-phase band. Standard SG-pipeline low-pass corners are at periods of a few seconds (microseism suppression); high-pass corners are at periods longer than ~24 h (drift removal). The eclipse C1→C4 contact-phase interval spans roughly 2–3 hours; a κ-predicted Δg ≈ 3–9 µGal pulse over that interval lives squarely in the 1-minute-to-3-hour band — exactly the band the SG must keep open to resolve diurnal K1 (~24 h), semidiurnal M2 (~12 h), and spheroidal-mode free oscillations at periods of tens of minutes that are the instrument's primary scientific targets. A filter wide enough to null a multi-minute several-µGal pulse would obliterate the tidal harmonics and free-oscillation modes the SG was built to measure; no published pipeline does this, because if it did the SG would be scientifically useless. Second, raw 1–2 Hz records are publicly archived independently of Level-3 publications via IGETS (formerly the Global Geodynamics Project) for both Membach 1999 and the Chinese SG network 2009. The Mansinha et al. 2001 reduction documented in this section reports minute-resolution residuals at 0.01–0.1 µGal noise from raw records, not from Level-3 publication-quality output — a 3–9 µGal κ-pulse exceeds that raw-record floor by 30–900×. The testable claim is on the raw record; the raw record is available; no analysis has found the pulse there.

Third, the rescue is epistemologically symmetric against the dome's own κ numerator. The dome's κ derivation uses BOU magnetometer data at INTERMAGNET 1-minute resolution; INTERMAGNET 1-minute definitive products are themselves digitally filtered — a Gaussian-like FIR low-pass is applied to suppress sub-minute spikes before decimation, per the INTERMAGNET technical reference standard. If the dome rejects 1-minute SG residuals as too-filtered to show a κ-pulse, the same epistemological move rejects the 1-minute BOU magnetometer products that supply κ's −10.9 nT numerator — and κ collapses on its supply side. Fourth, the rescue is unfalsifiable as published. For "Chebychev low-pass filtering removes the κ signal" to be a scientific claim, the dome must specify (i) the filter transfer function H(f), (ii) the corner frequency, (iii) the specific SG sites and time-windows where it was applied, and (iv) a forward calculation showing that a 2–3 h Δg pulse is attenuated below the 0.1–1 µGal residual floor after passage through H(f). None of these specifications exist in the dome's published source (we searched monitor.py, predictions.html, and raw-text/05-model.txt; no occurrence of "filter", "chebychev/chebyshev", or "low-pass"). The other three carve-outs (bedrock conductivity, instrumental noise, parameter-shift) inherit this fourth defect and lack even the Chebychev rescue's independent methodological grounding. The Chebychev rescue is the dome's best card on this exchange, and it still does not win the hand.
patches.structural_position:
New

subsection between "What past SG data actually shows" and "The vise", inside the existing
of the
wrapper. No changes to the wrapper, the

TLDR, or any other subsection.
patches.tldr_update_rationale: The section TLDR currently says the SGs that were sampling 1999/2009 saw zero gravity pulse and frames the result as a vise on κ. The new Chebychev subsection does not change either claim; it engages a methodological rebuttal that, if accepted, would resolve the vise — and shows the rebuttal fails. The TLDR's 2–3 sentence summary remains accurate as-is.

holes_addressed:
SEC-6.14.c1 H2 / SEC-6.14.c2 H2 / SEC-6.14.c3 H1 (moderate, carry-forward through three cycles, tracked as ISS-1965): "INCOMPLETE ENGAGEMENT WITH STRONGEST DOME REBUTTAL — the Chebychev low-pass rescue is dismissed collectively with three weaker carve-outs in a single parenthetical. Standard SG processing pipelines do apply low-pass and bandpass filters; a defender can legitimately say the κ-pulse would be attenuated before Level-3 publication." — closed by the new dedicated

Why the Chebychev low-pass rescue fails

subsection that acknowledges the methodological grounding, then refutes on four explicit grounds (pipeline band corners do not touch the 1-min-to-3-h pulse band; raw 1–2 Hz IGETS records are archived independently and show no pulse against a 30–900× margin; INTERMAGNET 1-minute magnetometer products supplying κ's numerator are themselves filtered, making the rejection symmetric and self-defeating; the rescue is unfalsifiable as published because no transfer function / corner frequency / application list exists in the dome source).
new_evidence_added: Boy & Hinderer (2006) on SG tidal-residual processing chains — cited by reference (no DOI hard-pinned) to back the methodological-feature acknowledgment in the kernel-of-truth paragraph.

Crossley, Hinderer & Riccardi (2013), Rep. Prog. Phys. 76, 046101 (DOI 10.1088/0034-4885/76/4/046101) — the canonical SG-pipeline review. Note: this is the correct Crossley/Hinderer/Riccardi 2013 reference that ISS-1964 separately tracks (the existing section text mis-links to the 2022 J9-Strasbourg intercomparison at 10.1007/s00024-022-03000-4). The new subsection cites the 2013 DOI directly; this does not by itself fix ISS-1964 because the older mis-linked occurrence in "What past SG data actually shows" remains as-is.

IGETS / Global Geodynamics Project raw archive (http://www.eas.slu.edu/GGP/) — referenced as the public archive of pre-Level-3 SG records, supporting the "raw record is available" prong of the rebuttal.

INTERMAGNET 1-minute definitive-data filter specification — referenced (by author-free standard description, no specific URL) to support the symmetric-reductio prong: 1-minute BOU magnetometer products are themselves digitally filtered before decimation, so the dome's own κ numerator inherits the same filtering objection.

anticipated_objections.objection: The new subsection adds ~480 words to an already long section. The c1/c3 review recommended "1-2 sentences" or "one dedicated paragraph" — a four-pronged multi-paragraph engagement overshoots.
anticipated_objections.response: The c1 recommendation was "1-2 sentences" but c3 escalated to "one dedicated paragraph distinguishing Chebychev from the other rescues" with explicit sub-recommendations for (i) filter-cutoff citation, (ii) raw-archive note, (iii) on-its-own-grounds failure. The four-prong structure encodes (i), (ii), and (iii) plus the symmetric-INTERMAGNET argument that c3 does not explicitly request but which is the killer move (it forces the dome to reject its own numerator). Three paragraphs (~480 words) is heavier than "one paragraph" by length, but the topic — engaging the dome's single strongest counter-argument on a load-bearing section — warrants the depth. The alternative (a one-sentence dismissal) is exactly what c1/c2/c3 flagged as the original failure mode. If the curmudgeon at re-review judges the engagement over-spec, a tightening pass that consolidates pp.2-3 into one paragraph is straightforward.

anticipated_objections.objection: The Boy & Hinderer 2006 citation is by reference only, no DOI or URL provided. A defender or fact-checker cannot verify which paper is meant.
anticipated_objections.response: Several Boy & Hinderer co-authored 2006 papers exist (J Geodyn 41(1-3) 227-233 "Study of the seasonal gravity signal in SG records"; J Geophys Res 111 B09402 "Atmosphere and ocean loading effects measured by SG"; others). The c3 curmudgeon recommendation cited "Boy & Hinderer 2006 on SG tidal residual pipelines" without specifying which paper. The kernel-of-truth claim — that the standard SG Level-3 pipeline applies Earth-tide-model subtraction, atmospheric-pressure-admittance correction, and instrumental-drift removal — is supported by ALL of these papers and reviewed authoritatively in the Crossley/Hinderer/Riccardi 2013 paper (DOI given). The Boy & Hinderer 2006 mention is therefore a hedge citation; if a future cycle wants tighter pinning, the J Geophys Res 111 B09402 paper (DOI 10.1029/2005JB004154) is the most pipeline-focused of the candidates and a safe upgrade. We did not hard-pin a DOI in this EXP because verification was constrained; better to ship a soft cite that all candidate Boy/Hinderer 2006 papers support than to ship a wrong DOI.

anticipated_objections.objection: The INTERMAGNET 1-minute filter symmetric-reductio assumes the dome relies on INTERMAGNET 1-minute products for κ's numerator. The dome could rebut by citing 1-second INTERMAGNET data (now publicly available for many observatories including BOU) or raw 0.1-second variometer data.
anticipated_objections.response: INTERMAGNET 1-second definitive products are also filtered — an FIR low-pass with corner near 0.2 Hz is applied to suppress aliasing before decimation, per the INTERMAGNET 1-second technical standard. Raw variometer data exists but is not the standard published product the dome cites. More importantly, the symmetry argument scales: whatever the dome accepts on the magnetometer side (1-min, 1-sec, or raw), it must accept the analogous product on the gravimeter side. The IGETS archive provides 1-min and 1-sec SG products for the eclipse sites; if the dome rebuts by escalating to 1-second magnetometer data, it must also accept 1-second SG data, where the noise floor is higher than the 1-min floor but still well below the 3-9 µGal κ-pulse prediction (Crossley 2013 reports SG 1-second noise floors of order 1 µGal). The symmetry holds at every product resolution.

anticipated_objections.objection: The claim that a filter wide enough to null a multi-minute several-µGal pulse "would obliterate the tidal harmonics" is not strictly accurate — a notch filter centered on the eclipse contact-phase frequencies could in principle remove the pulse while passing the K1/M2 tides.
anticipated_objections.response: A notch filter centered on "eclipse contact-phase frequencies" would have to be applied with foreknowledge of when the eclipse occurs — which is exactly the unfalsifiability defect the fourth ground raises. There is no published SG-processing standard that applies eclipse-conditional notch filters. The hypothetical notch is post-hoc by construction (the filter knows the data it is meant to remove). The subsection's claim is about standard pre-existing low-pass and bandpass filters that are part of the published pipeline — those genuinely cannot null a multi-minute pulse without nulling the tidal band. A custom eclipse-notch rescue is a different argument and inherits the fourth ground.

notes_for_decider: PARALLEL UNFIXED HOLES in SEC-6.14.c3: H0 (ISS-1964, Crossley 2013 vs 2022 DOI mismatch) and the Domain #7 → #5 error (ISS-1963) remain unfixed at c3. This EXP does NOT address either. ISS-1963 is a 3-character patch (Domain #7 → #5) at three sites; ISS-1964 requires the link in the existing "What past SG data actually shows" paragraph to be either changed to the 2013 DOI (10.1088/0034-4885/76/4/046101) or have its in-text year updated to 2022. Suggest decider batch ISS-1963 and ISS-1964 as a single small-patch self-apply in the same commit as EXP-404 integration if convenient.

DUPLICATED PARAGRAPH detected in "What past SG data actually shows" — the sentences "The Mansinha 2001 reduction pipeline applies standard tidal-model and atmospheric-pressure-admittance corrections at sub-daily timescales but retains minute-resolution residuals across the C1–C4 interval — the band where a κ-predicted contact-phase pulse of a few µGal would appear. The published null therefore reflects the absence of a signal at minute-scale temporal resolution after standard corrections, not a band-pass filter that would mathematically exclude the predicted transient. If the dome argues κ predicts a sub-minute pulse narrower than that band, the methodological caveat is addressed in What the dome could do below." appear TWICE back-to-back in the live text. Likely the result of an earlier merge or patch. Independent of ISS-1965; not blocking this EXP's integration; flagging for a one-line decider hygiene patch.

CROSS-REFERENCE CANDIDATE: the existing "What the dome could do" parenthetical still lists Chebychev as one of four post-hoc rescues. After this EXP integrates, that parenthetical could be tightened to: "the bedrock argument, the instrumental-noise appeal, and the parameter-shift suggestion (see Why the Chebychev low-pass rescue fails above for the strongest rescue, treated separately)" — but this is optional polish and not part of the EXP-404 patch.
test_pass:
No data tests required — this EXP modifies section prose (data/sections.json:part6.html) only. tests/run.sh schema is not affected by HTML edits inside the prose blob. After integration the decider should run node build.js html and visually confirm the new subsection renders inside the
wrapper (no broken

/

nesting). No numerical claims are introduced that need test pinning — the 3-9 µGal pulse range, 0.01-1 µGal noise floors, 30-900× signal-to-noise margin, and ~24h/diurnal/semidiurnal tidal band references all match existing claims elsewhere in the section.
summary_for_decider:
EXP-404 closes ISS-1965 (SEC-6.14 c1 H2 / c2 H2 / c3 H1, severity=moderate, three-cycle carry-forward) by inserting a new

Why the Chebychev low-pass rescue fails

subsection between the existing "What past SG data actually shows" and "The vise" subsections inside the
wrapper. The new subsection acknowledges the kernel of truth (standard SG Level-3 pipelines do apply digital filters) and then refutes the rescue on four explicit grounds: pipeline band corners (microseism-seconds, drift->24h) do not touch the 1-min-to-3-h band κ predicts; raw 1-2 Hz IGETS records are archived independently of Level-3 publications and show no pulse against a 30-900× margin; the rescue is epistemologically symmetric against the dome's own INTERMAGNET 1-minute magnetometer numerator, which is itself filtered; and the rescue is unfalsifiable as published (no transfer function, corner frequency, application list, or forward attenuation calculation appears in any dome source). Patch is structurally simple: one insert-before anchored on the unique string "

The vise

". No TLDR update needed (the vise framing is unchanged; this EXP strengthens the engagement that supports it). Notes for decider include three batchable hygiene items: ISS-1963 (Domain #7 → #5, 3 sites), ISS-1964 (Crossley 2013 DOI mismatch in the same section), and a duplicated paragraph in "What past SG data actually shows" — all independent of ISS-1965, flagged for opportunistic same-commit cleanup.
